**Balmain** is a [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") in the [Inner West](/wiki/Inner_West "Inner West")[[3]](#cite_note-3) of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), New South Wales, Australia. Balmain is located two kilometres (1+1⁄4 miles) west of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district"), in the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of the [Inner West Council](/wiki/Inner_West_Council "Inner West Council").

It is located on the Balmain peninsula surrounded by [Port Jackson](/wiki/Port_Jackson "Port Jackson"), adjacent to the suburbs of [Rozelle](/wiki/Rozelle "Rozelle") to the south-west, [Birchgrove](/wiki/Birchgrove,_New_South_Wales "Birchgrove, New South Wales") to the north-west, and [Balmain East](/wiki/Balmain_East "Balmain East") to the east. [Iron Cove](/wiki/Iron_Cove "Iron Cove") sits on the western side of the peninsula, with [White Bay](/wiki/White_Bay_(New_South_Wales) "White Bay (New South Wales)") on the south-east side and [Mort Bay](/wiki/Mort%27s_Dock "Mort's Dock") on the north-east side.

Traditionally [blue collar](/wiki/Blue-collar_worker "Blue-collar worker"), Balmain was where the industrial roots of the [trade unionist](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") movement began. It has become established in Australian [working-class culture](/wiki/Working-class_culture "Working-class culture") and history, due to being the place where the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") formed in 1891 and its social history and status is of high cultural significance to both Sydney and New South Wales. Today, the ALP contends with the [Australian Greens](/wiki/Australian_Greens "Australian Greens") for political prominence in Balmain, and [Kobi Shetty](/wiki/Kobi_Shetty "Kobi Shetty") of the Greens holds the State [seat of Balmain](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Balmain "Electoral district of Balmain").

In the 21st century however, Balmain is often considered to be an affluent area. According to the 2021 Australian Bureau of Statistics, Balmain had a Median weekly household income of $3,039,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and a 2021 article on the [Domain](/wiki/Domain_Group "Domain Group") website described Balmain as a Harbourside version and cheaper version of [Eastern Suburb](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Balmain%2C_New_South_Wales_-_Darling_St_c1888.jpg/250px-Balmain%2C_New_South_Wales_-_Darling_St_c1888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balmain,_New_South_Wales_-_Darling_St_c1888.jpg)

[Darling Street](/wiki/Darling_Street "Darling Street") around 1888

Prior to European settlement, the area was inhabited by indigenous [Aboriginal Australian](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians"), [Gadigal](/wiki/Cadigal "Cadigal") and [Wangal](/wiki/Wangal "Wangal") people. Stories from early settlers in the area tell of how the local indigenous people used to hunt [kangaroo](/wiki/Kangaroo "Kangaroo") by driving them through the bushy peninsula, down the hill to Peacock Point at the east end, where they were killed.

The area now known as Balmain was part of a 220-hectare (550-acre) grant to [colonial](/wiki/Colonial_Australia "Colonial Australia") surgeon [William Balmain](/wiki/William_Balmain "William Balmain") (1762–1803) made in 1800 by [Governor John Hunter](/wiki/John_Hunter_(Royal_Navy_officer) "John Hunter (Royal Navy officer)"). A year later, Balmain transferred his entire holding to settle a debt to [John Borthwick Gilchrist](/wiki/John_Borthwick_Gilchrist "John Borthwick Gilchrist") before returning to Scotland. The legality of the land transfer from Balmain to Gilchrist for only 5 [shillings](/wiki/Shilling "Shilling") was challenged by Balmain's descendants and further development of the area was blocked. The area subsequently became known as Gilchrist's place, though court documents refer to the area as the Balmain Estate.

During the many years of legal challenges, the land was leased for farming and cattle purposes. In 1814 the adjacent homestead of [Birchgrove](/wiki/Birchgrove,_New_South_Wales "Birchgrove, New South Wales") was sold to [Roland Warpole Loane](/wiki/Roland_Warpole_Loane "Roland Warpole Loane"), a merchant and settler descended from a family of English landlords. One hundred acres on the adjoining Balmain estate were leased to Loane.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Lever_Brothers_Balmain_-_Feb_1939.jpg/250px-Lever_Brothers_Balmain_-_Feb_1939.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lever_Brothers_Balmain_-_Feb_1939.jpg)

[Lever Brothers Factory](/wiki/Lever_Brothers_Factory "Lever Brothers Factory") 1939

In 1833, Gilchrist transferred [power of attorney](/wiki/Power_of_attorney "Power of attorney") to Frederick Parbury. When Loane's lease finally expired in 1836 and the land retrieved from his possession, Parbury commissioned surveyor John Armstrong to sub-divide the land into six parcels. Three parcels were sold to Thomas Hyndes in 1837. The area was rapidly sub-divided and developed during the 1840s and by 1861 had been divided into the well populated eastern suburb of Balmain and the sparsely populated western area, extending to the gates of [Callan Park](/wiki/Callan_Park "Callan Park"), known as Balmain West.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/City_Subdivision_Balmain%2C_Darling_St%2C_Elliott_St%2C_Terry_St%2C_Claremont_St%2C_1841-1960.jpg/250px-City_Subdivision_Balmain%2C_Darling_St%2C_Elliott_St%2C_Terry_St%2C_Claremont_St%2C_1841-1960.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Subdivision_Balmain,_Darling_St,_Elliott_St,_Terry_St,_Claremont_St,_1841-1960.jpg)

Early City Subdivision Balmain, Darling St, Elliott St, Terry St, Claremont St

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Industry")]

The peninsula changed rapidly during the 1800s and became one of the premier industrial centres of Sydney. Industries clustered around Mort Bay included shipbuilding, a metal foundry, engineering, [boilermaking](/wiki/Boilermaker "Boilermaker") and the [Mort's Dock & Engineering Company](/wiki/Mort%27s_Dock_%26_Engineering_Company "Mort's Dock & Engineering Company") works which opened in 1855—in 1958 Mort's Dock closed and is the site of Mort Bay Park.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Increasing industrialisation at Balmain created a demand for cheap housing. This was satisfied by the dock owners selling small blocks of land to entrepreneurs who then built tiny cottages and rented them to the workers. The [Balmain Reservoir](/wiki/Balmain_Reservoir "Balmain Reservoir") was built in 1915.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Rozelletram.jpg/250px-Rozelletram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rozelletram.jpg)

An [electric tram](/wiki/Trams_in_Sydney "Trams in Sydney") at [Balmain East ferry wharf](/wiki/Balmain_East_ferry_wharf "Balmain East ferry wharf") in 1951

[Lever Brothers Factory](/wiki/Lever_Brothers_Factory "Lever Brothers Factory"), owned by the British parent company, opened in 1895.

### Colliery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colliery")]

A coal mine was opened in 1897 beside what is now Birchgrove Public School by an English company. The winding engine was said to be the largest in the southern hemisphere. However, with the endless labour disputes,[[8]](#cite_note-Cessnock_Eagle_1933,_p.1-8) Sydney Collieries Limited took over ownership. A further new shaft was sunk in 1904. From the bottom of the shafts a decline led down to a seam of coal situated under the harbour between [Ballast Point](/wiki/Ballast_Point,_New_South_Wales "Ballast Point, New South Wales") and [Goat Island](/wiki/Goat_Island_(Port_Jackson) "Goat Island (Port Jackson)").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Because of the availability of the coal, [Balmain Power Station](/wiki/Balmain_Power_Station "Balmain Power Station") was erected in stages from 1909. However, there were major industrial disputes in the 1920s and calls were made by some shareholders in 1928 to close the mine.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The following year the colliery still employed 299 miners.[[11]](#cite_note-11) By 1930 the colliery owners had given up and it had been taken over by the Balmain Coal Contracting Company, established by the Miner's Federation[[12]](#cite_note-12) to keep the pit operational, to no avail as disputes continued. On 13 October 1930 the Miners' Lodge declared the mine "blacked" as well as the manager, whose dismissal they demanded.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The colliery closed in 1931. In 1933 it was proposed to extract natural gas for commercial use from the now disused mine.[[8]](#cite_note-Cessnock_Eagle_1933,_p.1-8) In 1936 calls were made for the Sydney City Council to take over the mine to no avail.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

One local waterman who benefited from the Balmain coalmine was Bob Miller, who resided at 102 Glassop Street. He purchased a small tug and barge and secured a contract to remove the mine tailings, which he on sold to local councils as fill for a handsome profit. This was the beginning of the [RW Miller](/wiki/RW_Miller "RW Miller") industrial conglomerate. By 1924 his family had moved to Louisa Road.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Tramway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tramway")]

The opening of the tramway in the 1920s further established Balmain and it gained a reputation as a rough working-class area of Sydney. A large influx of immigrants boosted Balmain's population in the 1950s.

### Gentrification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gentrification")]

Gentrification of Balmain began in the 1960s as industry waned. Balmain's desirability to the middle class was due in part to its waterfront location and proximity to Sydney's CBD. The Balmain Association was formed in 1965.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Increasing property values and waterfront development continued to push the suburb's remaining industry out. In 1996, the Lever Brothers site became a series of apartment complexes with a handful of original buildings preserved. The power station was demolished in 1998 to make way for apartments. However, many aspects of Balmain's industrial past have been retained as heritage.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demographics")]

At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), the population of Balmain was 10,454.[[25]](#cite_note-census2021-25) At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), it had a population of 10,453.[[26]](#cite_note-census2016-26)

In 2021, 64.8% of people were born in Australia. The next most common countries of birth were England 9.1%, New Zealand 2.8%, United States 1.7%, Ireland 1.3% and China 1.2%. 83.4% of people only spoke English at home. Other languages spoken at home included Mandarin 1.5% and Italian at 1.2%. The most common responses for religion in Balmain were no religion 51.9%, Catholic 20.7%, Anglican 9.9%, not stated 5.3% and Eastern Orthodox 2.2%. 37.6% of dwellings were flats, units or apartments, 39.9% were semi-detached terraced houses or townhouses and 21.8% were separate houses.[[25]](#cite_note-census2021-25)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Commercial area")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Balmain-Shopping-Area.jpg/250px-Balmain-Shopping-Area.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balmain-Shopping-Area.jpg)

Balmain features an abundance of [alfresco](/wiki/Al_fresco_dining "Al fresco dining") cafes and dining establishments, giving a prominent dining culture to the area

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Balmain_Working_Mens_Institute2.JPG/250px-Balmain_Working_Mens_Institute2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Balmain_Working_Mens_Institute2.JPG)

View of the Sydney Harbour Bridge from East Balmain, [Barangaroo Reserve](/wiki/Barangaroo,_New_South_Wales#Barangaroo_Reserve "Barangaroo, New South Wales") is in the foreground

[Darling Street](/wiki/Darling_Street "Darling Street"), Balmain's main thoroughfare, features boutique shops, quality restaurants and cafes alongside old [drinking establishments](/wiki/Bar_(establishment) "Bar (establishment)"). Landmarks on this street include the Post Office and Court House, alongside Balmain Town Hall, the historic Westpac Bank, Balmain Fire Station and Balmain Working Men's Institute. Other commercial developments are scattered throughout the suburb. The headquarters of the NSW Water Police moved to Cameron Cove in Balmain in late 2007.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/2_Balmain_Ferry_at_the_Balmain_Shipyard.JPG/250px-2_Balmain_Ferry_at_the_Balmain_Shipyard.JPG)](/wiki/File:2_Balmain_Ferry_at_the_Balmain_Shipyard.JPG)

Balmain Shipyard

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Balmain_Ferry_Wharf_in_2024.jpg/250px-Balmain_Ferry_Wharf_in_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balmain_Ferry_Wharf_in_2024.jpg)

Balmain Ferry Wharf

Balmain has several ferry wharves including Thames Street [Balmain](/wiki/Balmain_ferry_wharf "Balmain ferry wharf") serviced by the [Cockatoo Island ferry services](/wiki/Cockatoo_Island_ferry_services "Cockatoo Island ferry services"), Elliot Street, [Balmain West](/wiki/Balmain_West_ferry_wharf "Balmain West ferry wharf") and Darling Street, [Balmain East](/wiki/Balmain_East_ferry_wharf "Balmain East ferry wharf") serviced by the [Cross Harbour ferry services](/wiki/Cross_Harbour_ferry_services "Cross Harbour ferry services"). Services run to [Circular Quay](/wiki/Circular_Quay_ferry_wharf "Circular Quay ferry wharf"). [Transdev Sydney Ferries](/wiki/Transdev_Sydney_Ferries "Transdev Sydney Ferries")' maintenance and repair base is at Balmain Shipyard.

Balmain's road network feeds into three main roads—Darling Street, Beattie Street and Montague/Mullen Street. These streets have limited speeds, typically 40 km/h and are all [single carriageway](/wiki/Single_carriageway "Single carriageway") with parallel parking. Due to the geography of the peninsula, all of these roads feed into Victoria Road and the Western Distributor. Sections of Balmain were to be demolished to make for the North-West Expressway but this was prevented after green bans were placed by the NSW Builders Labourers Federation.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Trams once ran all the way down Darling Street to the wharf at Balmain East. Due to the very steep incline at the bottom of the street, the trams used a complex 'dummy' counterweight system constructed under the road surface. The trams were pushed up the steep hill by the dummy, and rode the dummy on the way down to safely descend the hill. [Transit Systems](/wiki/Transit_Systems_NSW "Transit Systems NSW") bus services that service Balmain are:

* 433 Balmain to [Railway Square](/wiki/Railway_Square "Railway Square").[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* 441 Birchgrove to [Art Gallery of New South Wales](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_New_South_Wales "Art Gallery of New South Wales"),[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* 442 Balmain to the [Queen Victoria Building](/wiki/Queen_Victoria_Building "Queen Victoria Building")[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* 445 Balmain to [Campsie](/wiki/Campsie,_New_South_Wales "Campsie, New South Wales")[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Culture")]

The [Balmain bug](/wiki/Balmain_bug "Balmain bug") (*Ibacus peronii*), a type of [slipper lobster](/wiki/Slipper_lobster "Slipper lobster") commonly eaten in Sydney, is named after Balmain.

### Historic hotels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Historic hotels")]

Balmain is home to many historic hotels, including the [Cat and Fiddle Hotel](/w/index.php?title=Cat_and_Fiddle_Hotel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cat and Fiddle Hotel (page does not exist)"), [Cricketer's Arms Hotel](/w/index.php?title=Cricketer%27s_Arms_Hotel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cricketer's Arms Hotel (page does not exist)"), [Dick's Hotel](/wiki/Dick%27s_Hotel "Dick's Hotel"), [Dry Dock Hotel](/wiki/Dry_Dock_Hotel "Dry Dock Hotel"), [Exchange Hotel](/wiki/Exchange_Hotel_(Balmain) "Exchange Hotel (Balmain)"), [Forth & Clyde Hotel](/wiki/Forth_%26_Clyde_Hotel "Forth & Clyde Hotel"), [Kent Hotel](/wiki/Kent_Hotel "Kent Hotel"), Unity Hall Hotel, London Hotel, Mort Bay Hotel, Norfolk Pines Hotel, Pacific Hotel, [Royal Oak Hotel](/wiki/Royal_Oak_Hotel "Royal Oak Hotel"), [Shipwright's Arms Hotel](/wiki/Shipwright%27s_Arms_Hotel "Shipwright's Arms Hotel"), [Star Hotel](/wiki/Star_Hotel,_Balmain "Star Hotel, Balmain"), Town Hall Hotel, [Volunteer Hotel](/wiki/Volunteer_Hotel "Volunteer Hotel") and the West End Hotel.

The [Riverview Hotel](/wiki/Riverview_Hotel,_Balmain "Riverview Hotel, Balmain") is a heritage-listed corner building built in 1880 in the [Arts and Crafts](/wiki/Australian_architectural_styles#Arts_and_Crafts "Australian architectural styles") style.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Between 1888 and 1913 the pub was named Bergin's Hotel after the publican Joseph Bergin. Australian swimming champion [Dawn Fraser](/wiki/Dawn_Fraser "Dawn Fraser") was publican of the Riverview from 1978 to 1983.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Pop culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pop culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Exchange_Hotel_Balmain_12.JPG/250px-Exchange_Hotel_Balmain_12.JPG)](/wiki/File:Exchange_Hotel_Balmain_12.JPG)

[Exchange Hotel, Beattie Street](/wiki/Exchange_Hotel_(Balmain) "Exchange Hotel (Balmain)")

Numerous phrases have been used to describe the suburb and its inhabitants, including "Balmain boys don't cry" (former NSW Premier [Neville Wran](/wiki/Neville_Wran "Neville Wran") at the Street Royal Commission); "You can take the boy out of Balmain, but you can't take Balmain out of the boy" (Unknown); "There are only two types of men in this world: those who were born in Balmain and those who wish they were" (a Police Commissioner of New South Wales). Australian Prime Minister [Paul Keating](/wiki/Paul_Keating "Paul Keating") commented on the suburb's gentrification by using the term "Basket weavers of Balmain".
Until the 1970s, older Balmain people would refer to travel into the city centre as "going to Sydney". The Post Office/Court/Police building and vicinity is generally referred to as the "Town Hall", with the Post Office clock often called the Town Hall clock. This was probably due to the Post Office & Court Building being built in between the earlier Town Hall & Town Hall Hotel.

In the mid-1960s Balmain was the setting for the popular [Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") situation comedy series *[My Name's McGooley, What's Yours?](/wiki/My_Name%27s_McGooley,_What%27s_Yours%3F "My Name's McGooley, What's Yours?")*, starring [Gordon Chater](/wiki/Gordon_Chater "Gordon Chater"), [John Meillon](/wiki/John_Meillon "John Meillon") and [Judi Farr](/wiki/Judi_Farr "Judi Farr").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Balmain was the setting for the 1994 Australian film *[The Sum of Us](/wiki/The_Sum_of_Us_(film) "The Sum of Us (film)")*, which starred [Jack Thompson](/wiki/Jack_Thompson_(actor) "Jack Thompson (actor)"), [John Polson](/wiki/John_Polson "John Polson") and [Russell Crowe](/wiki/Russell_Crowe "Russell Crowe").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The former Pacific Hotel (from 2019, now converted into a residential property) - as well as several other locations in Balmain - were used extensively as the set of the Australian television [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera"), *[E Street](/wiki/E_Street_(television_show) "E Street (television show)")*.

### Parks and reserves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Parks and reserves")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/1._east_balmain_park_on_mort_bay_syd_harbour.jpg/250px-1._east_balmain_park_on_mort_bay_syd_harbour.jpg)](/wiki/File:1._east_balmain_park_on_mort_bay_syd_harbour.jpg)

Public open space on the shores of Mort Bay on the Balmain, Balmain East border

Balmain has a number of parks including Gladstone Park, Birrung Park and White Bay Park. The wider peninsula has many more parks in close proximity, particularly along the foreshores.
In earlier times Punch Park was the goto place for park footy (rugby league), though it was always referred to by the boys then as Punch's Park

### Sport and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sport and recreation")]

Balmain is home to the [Balmain Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") district rugby league football club that is now represented in the NRL by [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") club. The club was formed in mid-1999 by a joint venture between the Balmain Tigers and the [Western Suburbs Magpies](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies") in preparation for the 2000 season.[[36]](#cite_note-wests_tigers-36) In 2005 the Tigers defeated the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") in the Grand Final to win the [premiership](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League").[[36]](#cite_note-wests_tigers-36)

The [Balmain Rugby Football Club](/wiki/Balmain_RFC "Balmain RFC"), founded in 1873, took part in the very first competition structure and in fact winning their first premiership in 1875. Players lost fighting in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") forced the club to merge with the Glebe "Dirty Reds" RUFC in 1919, to form the Glebe-Balmain RFC. As a merged club they had enormous success during the Twenties, winning four premierships. In 1931, as a depression project, Drummoyne Oval, as it is now known, was constructed on the site of a small oval which Glebe and Balmain had used for junior matches since 1892. To ensure longevity of tenure, and because there were just not enough sporting grounds in Sydney—with some reluctance—the Glebe-Balmain Club decided to change its name to the [Drummoyne District Rugby Football Club](/wiki/Drummoyne_DRFC "Drummoyne DRFC").[[37]](#cite_note-Drummoynerugby.com.au-37) It did so without giving up its long-held traditions, the scarlet jumpers of Glebe and its world-famous tag, "The Dirty Reds" and the black and gold of Balmain, colours still worn proudly by today's players in their socks. Drummoyne Rugby Club[[37]](#cite_note-Drummoynerugby.com.au-37) is still in existence and still upholds it proud history and traditions.

The suburb is also home to the [Balmain Australian Football Club](/wiki/Balmain_Australian_Football_Club "Balmain Australian Football Club"), a founding member in 1903 of the [Sydney Football League](/wiki/Sydney_AFL "Sydney AFL").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Balmain is also home to [Balmain FC](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers_FC "Balmain Tigers FC"), who are a semi-professional football club, playing in the [National Premier Leagues NSW 3](/wiki/National_Premier_Leagues_NSW_3 "National Premier Leagues NSW 3"). In 2015 they qualified for the Australia-wide [FFA Cup Round of 32](/wiki/2015_FFA_Cup#Round_of_32 "2015 FFA Cup") and were drawn against [A-League](/wiki/A-League "A-League") club [Melbourne Victory FC](/wiki/Melbourne_Victory_FC "Melbourne Victory FC") in which they were defeated 6–0 in front of 5,000 fans at [Leichhardt Oval](/wiki/Leichhardt_Oval "Leichhardt Oval").

[Balmain Sailing Club](/wiki/Balmain_Sailing_Club "Balmain Sailing Club") is located in nearby [Birchgrove](/wiki/Birchgrove,_New_South_Wales "Birchgrove, New South Wales") and is home to the annual Balmain Regatta.

[Balmain Rowing Club](/wiki/Balmain_Rowing_Club "Balmain Rowing Club") was formed in July 1882, and still occupies its original White Street location. The club adopted black and yellow colours from its formation, which soon became the district colours. The great [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") [sculling](/wiki/Sculling "Sculling") champion [Bill Beach](/wiki/Bill_Beach_(rower) "Bill Beach (rower)") had a close association with the club and wore their black and gold colours in competition.

## Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balmain,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Housing")]

The post-industrial [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") of Balmain has resulted in a suburb of considerable charm and interest where the modest, pretty houses command high prices. However, Balmain still retains a diverse mix of residents due to the [Housing Commission](/wiki/Housing_NSW "Housing NSW") unit blocks in the suburb.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Much of the suburb is a heritage conservation area and creative design is required to modernise the Victorian and Edwardian housing stock. In 2013 a contemporary Balmain house designed by architect [Harry Seidler](/wiki/Harry_Seidler "Harry Seidler") sold for $6m.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The [Tom Uren](/wiki/Tom_Uren "Tom Uren") House in Gilchrist Place was designed by [Richard Leplastrier](/wiki/Richard_Leplastrier "Richard Leplastrier").
